title: The political asymmetry of who is allowed to be tired
summary: Examine how exhaustion is distributed unequally across social positions, and argue that who is permitted to express tiredness — and be taken seriously — is a question of power.
topic: How the social permission to express and be believed in exhaustion reflects and reinforces existing hierarchies
prompt: Write a 600–800-word argumentative essay exploring the political dimension of tiredness: who is culturally permitted to express exhaustion, who is believed when they do, and how this asymmetry reflects broader distributions of power and legitimacy. Use at least two specific examples from different national or sectoral contexts (e.g. burnout recognition in professional vs. manual labor, the dismissal of maternal exhaustion in different cultural settings, or differential treatment of fatigue in post-conflict or migration contexts). Deploy inversion at least once, a cleft sentence, and a 'granted… even so…' concession. Avoid sympathy-based arguments; make a structural one.
hintStarters: Nowhere is the inequality of credibility more legible than in the differential reception of the claim 'I am exhausted'…
What is at stake in the politics of tiredness is not comfort but…
Granted, all demanding work produces fatigue. Even so,…

hintVocab.meaningVi: độ tin cậy nhận thức luận
tính chính đáng của lời than phiền
sự mệt mỏi bị phủ nhận
sự làm việc quá sức về mặt cấu trúc
lời than phiền có chứng chỉ
tu từ về khả năng phục hồi
sự mệt mỏi hậu thuộc địa
khối lượng công việc vô hình
nghỉ ngơi như sự kháng cự
sự công nhận chính trị về nỗi đau

sampleText:
Nowhere is the inequality of credibility more legible than in the differential reception of the claim 'I am exhausted.' This claim, uttered by a management consultant after a demanding quarter, enters a social world that has built entire institutions to receive it: wellness programmes, executive coaching, sabbatical leave policies, the diagnosis of burnout as a legitimate occupational phenomenon officially recognised by the World Health Organisation in 2019. The same claim, made by a domestic worker, a seasonal agricultural labourer, or a migrant care worker — and made with considerably more physiological justification — tends to enter a social world that has built no comparable infrastructure of belief.

The asymmetry is not incidental. It reflects the distribution of what the philosopher Miranda Fricker has called 'epistemic credibility' — the social capital that determines whether a speaker is believed. Those whose tiredness is taken seriously are, in the main, those whose cognitive and professional labour is already recognised as valuable. The investment banker who cannot sleep, the surgeon who describes the toll of consecutive operating days, the journalist who reports symptoms of vicarious trauma: these figures find themselves embedded in professional cultures that have developed a vocabulary for their exhaustion. Manual workers, domestic labourers, and care workers — whose physical and emotional output is often greater in absolute terms — find themselves instead embedded in cultures that valorise their endurance and frame their exhaustion as either expected or exaggerated.

It is precisely the medicalisation of professional fatigue — the creation of diagnoses, research programmes, and wellness industries oriented toward white-collar exhaustion — that has deepened this asymmetry rather than resolved it. By treating burnout as primarily a condition of knowledge workers, the diagnostic apparatus creates a threshold of legitimacy that excludes the forms of depletion most commonly experienced by those lower in occupational hierarchies. The farmworker who harvests strawberries for twelve hours in forty-degree heat does not receive a diagnosis; she receives a wage. The sleeplessness of the new mother in a society with no statutory parental leave, the accumulated fatigue of the migrant construction worker who sends money home and cannot afford to be visibly tired: these are not conditions the wellness industry was designed to address.

Granted, the recognition of professional burnout represents a genuine advance in occupational health, and the expansion of diagnostic categories to include psychological exhaustion has helped many people gain access to treatment and rest they would otherwise have been denied. Even so, the political consequences of selective medicalisation deserve scrutiny. When the language of burnout is available to some workers but not to others — when tiredness is taken as a sign of system failure in one context and as a sign of individual inadequacy in another — the result is not a more humane attitude toward exhaustion in general but the production of a credentialled complaint: one that is legitimate only for those who already possess the social standing to make it.

The gendered dimension of this asymmetry has been extensively documented, most forcefully in feminist scholarship on the 'second shift' — the unpaid domestic and care labour that falls disproportionately on women after paid employment ends. In Japan, where karoshi — death from overwork — has become a recognised phenomenon in white-collar professional settings, the equivalent term for the exhaustion of unpaid carers remains largely absent from public discourse. In Brazil, the exhaustion of the trabalhadora doméstica, the domestic worker who travels two hours each way to work in the households of the professional class, is structurally produced by the same economy that grants the professional class the resource to contemplate their own burnout.

The politics of tiredness, then, is not a matter of who suffers more — a comparison that is both impossible and beside the point. It is a matter of whose suffering is made legible, whose rest is treated as a right, and whose endurance is treated as a natural feature of their position rather than as evidence of a system extracting more from some people than it is willing to acknowledge. Rest, in this frame, is not only a physiological need; it is a political claim — and like all political claims, its reception depends on who is making it and from where.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Negative inversion with 'nowhere' to open with a strong diagnostic claim
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'Nowhere is the inequality of credibility more legible than in the differential reception of the claim «I am exhausted»' dùng đảo ngữ được kích hoạt bởi trạng từ phủ định 'nowhere'. Thay vì 'The inequality of credibility is nowhere more legible than…', đảo ngữ đặt 'nowhere' lên đầu và đảo ngữ 'is', tạo ra giọng điệu chứng minh: luận điểm được đưa ra trực tiếp như một quan sát, không phải như một câu hỏi.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The opening 'Nowhere is the inequality of credibility more legible than in the differential reception of the claim «I am exhausted»' uses inversion triggered by the negative adverb 'nowhere'. Rather than 'The inequality of credibility is nowhere more legible than…', the inversion fronts 'nowhere' and inverts the auxiliary 'is', producing a diagnostic register: the claim is presented as direct observation, not as a tentative hypothesis.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Cleft sentence to isolate the key mechanism
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'It is precisely the medicalisation of professional fatigue — the creation of diagnoses, research programmes, and wellness industries oriented toward white-collar exhaustion — that has deepened this asymmetry rather than resolved it.' dùng cấu trúc tách 'It is X that Y'. Ngoặc kép em-dash giải thích thêm về 'medicalisation' mà không làm mất cấu trúc câu. Cụm 'rather than resolved it' tạo ra sự đảo ngược kỳ vọng: điều có vẻ là giải pháp lại làm trầm trọng thêm vấn đề.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The cleft 'It is precisely the medicalisation of professional fatigue — the creation of diagnoses, research programmes, and wellness industries oriented toward white-collar exhaustion — that has deepened this asymmetry rather than resolved it.' uses 'It is X that Y'. The em-dash parenthetical elaborates 'medicalisation' without breaking the cleft. The phrase 'rather than resolved it' creates an expectation reversal: what appears to be a solution has worsened the problem.

sampleTranslationVi:
Không ở đâu sự bất bình đẳng về độ tin cậy rõ ràng hơn trong sự tiếp nhận khác biệt của tuyên bố 'Tôi kiệt sức.' Tuyên bố này, được phát biểu bởi một nhà tư vấn quản lý sau một quý vất vả, bước vào một thế giới xã hội đã xây dựng toàn bộ tổ chức để tiếp nhận nó: các chương trình sức khỏe, huấn luyện điều hành, chính sách nghỉ phép dài hạn, chẩn đoán kiệt sức như một hiện tượng nghề nghiệp hợp pháp được WHO công nhận chính thức năm 2019. Tuyên bố tương tự, được đưa ra bởi một lao động gia đình, một lao động nông nghiệp theo mùa, hay một người chăm sóc di cư — và được đưa ra với biện hộ sinh lý học đáng kể hơn nhiều — có xu hướng bước vào một thế giới xã hội không có cơ sở hạ tầng niềm tin tương đương.

Sự bất đối xứng không phải là ngẫu nhiên. Nó phản ánh sự phân phối cái mà triết gia Miranda Fricker gọi là 'độ tin cậy nhận thức luận' — vốn xã hội xác định xem người nói có được tin hay không. Những người có sự mệt mỏi được xem trọng chủ yếu là những người mà lao động nhận thức và nghề nghiệp của họ đã được công nhận là có giá trị. Lao động thủ công, lao động gia đình và người lao động chăm sóc — có đầu ra thể chất và cảm xúc thường lớn hơn về mặt tuyệt đối — lại thấy mình được nhúng vào các nền văn hóa tôn vinh sức chịu đựng của họ và khung hóa sự kiệt sức của họ là hoặc được kỳ vọng hoặc bị phóng đại.

Chính xác là sự y tế hóa của mệt mỏi nghề nghiệp — sự tạo ra các chẩn đoán, chương trình nghiên cứu, và ngành công nghiệp sức khỏe hướng đến kiệt sức cổ trắng — đã đào sâu sự bất đối xứng này thay vì giải quyết nó. Bằng cách coi kiệt sức chủ yếu là tình trạng của người lao động tri thức, bộ máy chẩn đoán tạo ra một ngưỡng chính đáng loại trừ các dạng kiệt quệ phổ biến nhất của những người thấp hơn trong hệ thống phân cấp nghề nghiệp. Người nông dân thu hoạch dâu tây mười hai giờ trong thời tiết bốn mươi độ không nhận được chẩn đoán; cô ấy nhận được tiền lương.

Cứ cho là sự công nhận kiệt sức nghề nghiệp là một tiến bộ thực sự trong sức khỏe nghề nghiệp. Dù vậy, hậu quả chính trị của việc y tế hóa có chọn lọc cần được xem xét. Khi ngôn ngữ kiệt sức có sẵn cho một số người lao động nhưng không phải tất cả — khi sự mệt mỏi được xem là dấu hiệu thất bại của hệ thống trong một bối cảnh và là dấu hiệu không đủ năng lực của cá nhân trong bối cảnh khác — kết quả không phải là thái độ nhân đạo hơn đối với sự kiệt sức nói chung mà là sản xuất ra một lời than phiền có chứng chỉ.

Chiều cạnh giới tính của sự bất đối xứng này đã được ghi chép kỹ lưỡng, mạnh mẽ nhất trong nghiên cứu nữ quyền về 'ca làm việc thứ hai'. Ở Nhật Bản, nơi karoshi — chết vì làm việc quá sức — đã trở thành hiện tượng được công nhận trong các cơ quan chuyên nghiệp cổ trắng, thuật ngữ tương đương cho sự kiệt sức của người chăm sóc không được trả lương vẫn phần lớn vắng mặt trong diễn ngôn công cộng. Ở Brazil, sự kiệt sức của người lao động gia đình di chuyển hai giờ mỗi chiều đến làm việc trong các hộ gia đình chuyên nghiệp được tạo ra về mặt cấu trúc bởi chính nền kinh tế cho phép tầng lớp chuyên nghiệp suy ngẫm về kiệt sức của riêng họ.

Do đó, chính trị của sự mệt mỏi không phải là vấn đề ai đau khổ hơn. Đó là vấn đề về sự đau khổ của ai được hiển thị, ai có sự nghỉ ngơi được coi là quyền, và ai có sức chịu đựng được coi là đặc điểm tự nhiên của vị trí họ. Sự nghỉ ngơi, trong khuôn khổ này, không chỉ là nhu cầu sinh lý; đó là một yêu cầu chính trị — và như tất cả các yêu cầu chính trị, sự tiếp nhận của nó phụ thuộc vào ai đang đưa ra nó và từ đâu.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses negative inversion with 'nowhere' correctly?
options: Nowhere the inequality of credibility is more legible than in the reception of 'I am exhausted'.
The inequality of credibility nowhere more legible is than in the claim.
Nowhere is the inequality of credibility more legible than in the differential reception of the claim 'I am exhausted'.
Nowhere does the credibility of inequality more legible become.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Nowhere' triggers subject-auxiliary inversion: 'Nowhere is X' not 'Nowhere X is'.
mcQuestions.hint: The auxiliary 'is' must come immediately after 'nowhere'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'epistemic credibility' mean in this essay?
options: The ability to speak multiple languages
A medical certificate confirming exhaustion
The authority granted by professional qualifications
The social capital that determines whether a speaker is believed when they make a claim
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay defines 'epistemic credibility' as 'the social capital that determines whether a speaker is believed' — it is distributed unequally.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the definition in the second paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: The essay says the medicalisation of professional fatigue has 'deepened this asymmetry rather than resolved it'. What does this mean?
options: By framing burnout primarily as a condition of knowledge workers, the diagnostic apparatus excludes the fatigue of lower-status workers from the threshold of legitimacy
Medical research has proven that white-collar workers suffer more than manual workers
Doctors refuse to treat exhaustion in manual workers
The wellness industry only works for managers
mcQuestions.explanation: The medicalisation creates 'a threshold of legitimacy that excludes the forms of depletion most commonly experienced by those lower in occupational hierarchies.'
mcQuestions.hint: Think about who is included and who is excluded by the burnout diagnosis.

options: A complaint that has been verified by a doctor
A grievance that is only considered legitimate for those who already have sufficient social standing
A formal written complaint submitted to an employer
A complaint backed by legal documentation
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay defines a credentialled complaint as one 'that is legitimate only for those who already possess the social standing to make it' — credentials are social, not purely professional.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the definition following 'credentialled complaint'.

mcQuestions.prompt: The essay uses the example of Japan's karoshi to illustrate what point?
options: That Japan has the world's worst work culture
That karoshi should be legally banned
That a society can recognise professional exhaustion in men while the equivalent exhaustion of unpaid carers remains absent from public discourse
That Japanese women are more exhausted than Japanese men
mcQuestions.explanation: The Japan example contrasts the public recognition of karoshi with the absence of equivalent language for unpaid carers' exhaustion — both gendered and labour-category asymmetries.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at what karoshi is contrasted with in the same sentence.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the 'second shift' as referenced in the essay?
options: The night shift in manufacturing
A second job taken to supplement income
The second period of a work day after a lunch break
The unpaid domestic and care labour that falls disproportionately on women after paid employment ends
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay references feminist scholarship on the 'second shift — the unpaid domestic and care labour that falls disproportionately on women after paid employment ends.'
mcQuestions.hint: This is a well-known feminist concept — think about what work women do after their paid working day.

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, what is the central political question about tiredness?
options: Whose suffering is made legible, whose rest is treated as a right, and whose endurance is naturalised as part of their position
Who suffers most from physical exhaustion
Whether exhaustion should be classified as a medical condition
How many hours constitute dangerous overwork
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay states explicitly: 'It is a matter of whose suffering is made legible, whose rest is treated as a right, and whose endurance is treated as a natural feature of their position.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the paragraph beginning 'The politics of tiredness, then…'

options: Lying under oath in a court of law
The injustice that occurs when a speaker receives less credibility than they deserve due to their social identity
The unfair distribution of witness protection
Injustice arising from inaccurate testimony
mcQuestions.explanation: Testimonial injustice, a concept from Miranda Fricker's work referenced in the essay, is the injustice of having one's claims dismissed or disbelieved because of one's social identity.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'testimony' (what someone says) + 'injustice' (being treated unfairly).

mcQuestions.prompt: The essay's concession is that burnout recognition is a genuine advance. What counter-claim follows?
options: But burnout should not be treated as a medical condition at all
But the wellness industry is too expensive for most workers
But selective medicalisation has political consequences: it creates a credentialled complaint available only to those with social standing
But psychological exhaustion is less serious than physical exhaustion
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay grants the advance, then argues via 'Even so' that 'the political consequences of selective medicalisation deserve scrutiny' because it creates a legitimacy threshold based on social standing.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for what follows 'Even so' in the fourth paragraph.

options: Political speeches about national recovery
A psychological technique for managing stress
Rhetoric used to promote physical fitness
The cultural discourse that frames endurance of hardship as a virtue, typically applied to those whose suffering the system does not want to acknowledge
mcQuestions.explanation: Resilience rhetoric is the language of 'bouncing back' and enduring — the essay implies it is deployed selectively to frame the exhaustion of lower-status workers as expected endurance rather than systemic failure.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about who resilience is demanded of and why.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which correctly uses a cleft sentence to isolate the medicalisation mechanism?
options: It is precisely the medicalisation of professional fatigue that has deepened this asymmetry rather than resolved it.
The medicalisation of professional fatigue has deepened this asymmetry rather than resolved it.
Professional fatigue medicalisation has it precisely deepened.
What precisely deepened this asymmetry is to medicalise fatigue professionally.
mcQuestions.explanation: Only option C uses the correct 'It is X that Y' cleft structure with 'precisely' for ironic emphasis.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the 'It is… that…' cleft pattern.

options: Physical work in factories
Work that involves managing and producing emotional states in others, such as care work, teaching, or hospitality
Work done with great enthusiasm
Labour regulated by affective contracts
mcQuestions.explanation: Affective labour is work that involves emotional engagement — producing care, comfort, or emotional connection. It is often invisible and undervalued.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'affect' as emotion or feeling, combined with 'labour' as work.

mcQuestions.prompt: The essay ends by saying rest 'is a political claim'. What does this mean?
options: Governments should pass laws about rest
Rest should be a human right in international law
Like any political claim, who is entitled to rest and whose right to rest is recognised depends on power and social position
Political parties disagree about how much rest workers need
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues that 'its reception depends on who is making it and from where' — rest is not simply a biological need but a claim whose legitimacy is socially and politically distributed.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for what makes a claim 'political' in the essay's logic.

options: Overwork caused by an individual employee's poor time management
Overwork in the construction industry
Working more than the number of hours set by a union contract
Overwork that is produced by the structure of economic and social arrangements rather than by individual choice or poor management
mcQuestions.explanation: Structural overwork is produced by the economic system — wages that require long hours, lack of childcare, no parental leave — not by personal failings.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'structural' as relating to the system, not the individual.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's main argument?
options: The permission to express exhaustion and be believed is distributed according to social power, and the medicalisation of professional fatigue has reinforced rather than resolved this inequality.
Manual workers suffer more than office workers and should be compensated accordingly.
Burnout is not a real medical condition.
The wellness industry exploits workers financially.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay consistently argues that who is believed when they say they are exhausted reflects existing power hierarchies, and that selective medicalisation deepens rather than resolves the asymmetry.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the argument that combines power, credibility, and the consequences of selective recognition.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a time when your own tiredness was taken seriously — and a time when it was dismissed. What was different about the two situations: your role, the audience, the context, the type of work involved? What does this tell you about the social conditions under which exhaustion is granted legitimacy?
